speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 2. Today is February 18th, 1996. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld and I'm interviewing Mr. Harry Klein. OK. You were telling me about the day that you were taken from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put-- 90 people were put into one cattle car. And there was food, but there was no room to move about. It-- it was so limited and crying and screaming-- children were screaming. They couldn't sleep. And this went on for five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live for the five days in this car?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones that couldn't take it did not live. It was about 15, between 15 and 20 people that didn't make it in every car. And yet when we landed in Germany-- in Poland, the Germans counted those, too. They counted and recounted and made sure that every cattle car contained 90, not 89. Well, it was very hard. And the strong ones survived. Most of them did. But there were casualties right there and then in the cattle car. Five days without any air and so on, and so it was too hot. And you couldn't sleep. And you couldn't sleep because you couldn't sit. 90 people-- there was no room to move about. And screaming all the time. And the screaming was-- was justified, because children were screaming and they weren't attended to. The people couldn't do it. That's it. That went on for five days. We didn't really know what a concentration camp is or where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry, the five days, they constantly moved, or you stopped, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They may have stopped on and off. But we-- they didn't let us-- they didn't open the doors. Yeah. They stopped on and off, not for long, maybe an hour or two here and there. But as far as we were concerned, nothing changed. Nobody opened the door. They gave us-- as far as I remember, they gave us food for five days. Everybody had his own. Whether they could get to the food in time, because it was so much-- there was limited space for everybody. Even if you had the food, they may-- may-- some people may not have been able to eat it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: --or drink it. I-- as far as I remember, I ate part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have something to drink, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They gave-- now, water was very limited, but I had some, maybe one glass a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they stopped the cars to give you water?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stopped the cars and they gave us a little water. They brought it with canteens or whatever, containers, and gave us a little water. And then they continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And so you couldn't sleep, you couldn't stand-- sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't sleep. I couldn't sit. I ate some. And somehow-- somehow I arrived there. I don't know how. That was some experience. The people already knew that nothing is good. You know, you come from a nice home, from-- and the ghetto was the first step to dehumanize you. So the conditions in the ghetto compared with the cattle car, it was like heaven and hell. Well, anyway, the ones that had strong will survived. Very old people couldn't survive in that heat for five days, and no space, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that was on the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really didn't know anybody, because I was from a different town. I-- I was just a stranger in Ungvar. I'm from Munkacs. And I didn't really know these people. I only knew the people that were with me all along in-- in Ungvar, I lived with for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other people--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, they didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't go on the cars with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They did-- oh, wait a minute. No, they did not go in the same car. No. They went by alphabet, I think. They had different name, so I don't-- no, we didn't go together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I really didn't know many people. Or maybe-- maybe I knew one or two people in my car. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about five days we were struggling and in this car. And then, the train stopped. And there was a big sign, as far as I remember. They said Auschwitz. And we didn't know. We knew that we were in Poland, I don't know how we-- well, there was Polish signs on some railroad stations. We knew we were in Poland. And there was a big sign, Auschwitz. And we didn't have enough time to think. As soon as the train stopped, there was Jewish or non-Jewish prisoners with striped clothes, striped prison clothes, came up barking orders like SS officers. They'd been there before and they had been hardened people, you know. They saw a lot and they went through a lot. I figure in order to stay alive, they had to be like this. Like animals, they treated us. And they spoke in German, a few German commands, like, schnell means fast, fast. I mean, get out, fast. Move. And then they helped everybody down right away. And then they formed a group of 90. And the ones that didn't survive, they put them on the side. And they counted them and recounted them. SS officers counted. SS guards, not officers, sorry. SS guards counted the group. And they had papers to sign for their superiors that they received this and this carload number. Each car had a serial number, I assume. And everything was officially done. And then we marched. These people that came up, maybe four or five prisoners, marched us into Auschwitz. I don't really remember how long the march was, but I would assume one mile or maybe two miles. Two miles. Now, here is a-- here is a-- my notes say-- now, everybody heard about Dr. Mengele. He's the Angel of Death. He's the only one who showed people to the right or to the left. My notes don't say that. My notes say there was German SS officers, not one, that directed people this way or that way. We really didn't know what right means or the left means, but we saw they were separated. Some people were directed to go to the right. Some people to the left. Now, we found out later what it meant, and maybe in a day or two. Well anyway, I don't remember what side I was on, but on the side of the survivors, apparently.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the other side, the ones that were directed to the other side, like little children or very old people, they were taken directly to the gas chambers. They didn't bother taking them into one-- certain buildings. They-- they had their own-- their own special route directly to the gas chambers, where they were-- I figure, they were undressed and put to death within six, seven hours. But you didn't know that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not know. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day later, I-- I heard it from other people. Well, I was taken with the other people who were directed to the right. I was taken, as far as I remember, to a place where they shaved us. And-- and they gave-- no, no numbers. No. They shaved us. They took all our clothing away. And they announced, anybody-- no, I'm sorry. I'm going back to Hungary now. When we entered the train that was in Hungary, there was Hungarian police, which was against the law, I figure. And they said to us, anybody has a watch or any gold or money better give it to us or you'll be punished for it. Some people did, some people didn't. Some people took a chance. They didn't give it to them. And some people survived on account of a-- of a watch. Or later on, in a few months, for a watch, you could buy so much bread. And that-- that meant survival in many cases.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not surrender anything. I don't remember what I had. I had-- may have-- I may have had a gold ring. I had a watch and I had good shoes. The shoes-- the shoes saved me. Good new shoes, I had. And money-- Hungarian money meant nothing in Poland. The money didn't mean anything, because they had their own currency and so on. But jewelry meant a lot. So whatever I had, I kept. Now when we went to Auschwitz, these prisoners that accompanied us, and also the SS guards, said, you have to surrender everything. They knew about it, that some prisoners have. It's natural. Some people obeyed the order, because that order that was given in Hungary was against the law. The Germans had the jurisdiction over these people, not the Hungarians, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and here, there was really no choice, because you had to abandon all your clothing, all your suitcases, and you were naked. So what could you keep? Oh, the shoes I could keep. They let you keep shoes and only clothing. Nothing else. No wallets, no suitcases. The shoes-- and no shirts, nothing. So they gave you new clothes, one shirt and as far as I know, one pair of underwear. And they gave you-- they gave everybody a uniform, a jacket and a pair of pants.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember about socks. I don't remember. I may have-- I don't know. I don't know. And-- and then I think they were taken showers. I don't remember that. No. And then we were taken into the camp to a certain area. And we were stationed in front of a big barrack. And we were hanging around that. And then, we had to register. Everybody had to register by name, and so on. It took hours, hours, because the barracks had 800 people, or 900. You're talking 800 people to register names. It was about four desks there, or benches, four people working, making out lists and names, and so on. No numbers. The numbers were given later. And I figure the whole group didn't have numbers. The numbers are mainly given to people who were able to do work. All the others didn't get numbers. They went straight to the gas chambers. They didn't need any numbers. So why I didn't get a number is-- the reason is because my group didn't stay there too long in Auschwitz, so I figure they didn't need numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day that you registered, the 800 people, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: 800 people registered, and then they let them inside the barracks, which took around 800 people. And-- and it was bunks, three-tiered bunks. And the people occupied sleeping places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry, what did they give you? What did they ask you when they registered you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a name and from what town you came or from what ghetto. And that's as far as I know. Or maybe your parents' name, could be. That's it. No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after-- after you were registered?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name of the ghetto, what ghetto you were in. After we registered, not one-- we were standing on the side. When the whole group was registered, and they introduced us to a kapo, which is the head of the barracks. And over there in my note I have the exact German name for another leader of the-- and these-- these-- the kapo did not have to be German. In some areas-- and not in Auschwitz as well. Later on I see in my notes, there was-- I had one kapo who was Jewish-- was Jewish. But they had to be tough. They're very tough. And the kapos they had enough food, and they looked healthy. And they had clubs in their hands. Any little thing, they clubbed the people like nothing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this kapo in Auschwitz was a German, that's what you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: German or non-Jewish. They had Greek prisoners, political prisoners from all countries, non-Jewish. Somebody said anything about the Nazis that they didn't like, you wound up in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the kapo tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapo read from a paper the regulations of the camp. Anybody disobeying an order will be punished. Not spelled out, what a punishment is. Now as far as-- I don't remember the exact rules, but he mentioned the word appell. Appell means-- I don't know if it's a German word or Latin-- appell means that early in the morning, around 4:00 AM, they get the people out of the barracks and they chase them to a washroom. Washroom means in the open. And they had a little water, cold water running. And you had to wash up up to here. And that's without soap, or sometimes with soap, I don't remember. And that had to be done in the course of three, four minutes. And then, dress, line up in a-- a group of 800 had to be lined up in fives or tens, rows of tens-- and wait, and wait, and wait, for hours until an SS officer came and counted and recounted. Of course, he didn't get up at 4:00 AM, so we had to wait till 7:00 AM, sort of. And some days it was very cold. These-- these pajamas that they gave us, the striped-- it was very, very cold. And some people got sick just waiting. In fact, many of them died waiting in the appell. Not the first day, they were strong. They came from a-- I mean, in the course of a month, some people died right outside waiting for the SS officer to count them. That's how brutal they were. And-- the first few-- as far as I remember, I didn't do any work in Auschwitz, so we were hanging around. Yeah, early in the morning, the appell. And after they counted us-- we were not assigned work, yet. We were newcomers, so we were standing around. And I figure around 8:00 AM, they gave everybody a little coffee, just coffee, nothing else. All right. Coffee is better than nothing. I drank it. And I still had a little bread with me. I don't know. I smuggled it in. To this date-- my notes say I had bread for three days. How I could smuggle in, maybe inside the shoes, I'm not sure. I had bread with me that I brought.

speaker: interviewee
text: So lunch time was about around 1:00 or 2 o'clock. Here is the problem. Half of us couldn't eat it. It was soup with a certain smell and pieces of potato, potato peel. But I tried, and I knew I had to have-- I will have to eat, otherwise I'll die from hunger. The first day, I couldn't-- I couldn't eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So that wasn't all. They gave everybody a little plate, a deep plate with a-- and the next one. And then they used the same plate for the next group, because they didn't have enough plate for 900 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to go to the side, get rid of the contents, and smuggle myself in into a row to surrender the plate. If a guard sees me with a plate with no soup, there's something suspicious. Right away, there's a beating involved. So, that was the first day.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening, they-- I didn't-- I didn't think it was bread, but they-- they-- they had dinner. The dinner consisted of-- it says there in my note how many grams, very little. I think it was two slices. And they looked black or gray, like brick. And one small square of margarine or-- or jelly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I couldn't believe that this is bread. I never-- and later on, I found out that the German army gets the same bread. And I tried to eat that. The first day, I couldn't eat that either. I said, I couldn't touch it. So, I had no choice. I don't remember exactly what I did with the first portion. Then I took out bread that I had with me and I ate the first day. And second day, the same thing went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then about on the third day, I look at my stock. What I brought was almost all gone. And I saw all the other people that they all-- they all came from homes like me. And they-- little by little, they got used to it. There was no choice. Either eat it, or within a week or two weeks, you'll be dead. That's it. So little by little, I got used to it. And I-- and especially the bread, I-- I got to like it. I got hungry. And I-- and I saw many people, they didn't eat it all. They ate one slice at night and one slice in the morning with the bitter coffee, or whatever. So they divided it so they have it for two days, sort of. And this went on until on the third day, I-- I started to eat the soup, or what they called soup. But little by little, I got used to it. And this is how we spent the first week in Auschwitz, sitting in the sun and talking, and hoping that the war will be over, which was a dream at that time. It was in-- in the middle of '44, so it was very far from over. In the meantime, once in a while I met people from the same town marching by, doing some work. I asked about my sisters, and nobody knew. But my parents-- when I was a different transport, because I was from a different town, and nobody knew anything. But we met old-- haftling means prisoner in German. Haftling. H-A-F-T-L-I-N-G. We met all the prisoners, Jewish, possibly Polish people, have been there for two years. And they didn't encourage us at all. They-- they said, if your group is going to be shipped out of Auschwitz, you have a slight chance of survival. If your group-- but the percentage-- this guy was from Poland and he talked-- you know, he talked. He said, the-- all the newcomers are dreaming the war will be over. And by that time, there will be nothing left of you. You don't have to worry about that. So-- and he gave us a few tips how to obey orders, not to fight the kapo or blockalteste. That's a block leader. That is a German. Every barracks had a German in charge of the kapo. And blockalteste, that's the older, barracks older it's translated. And-- and he said to us, to me and a few more people, whatever they say, obey. Otherwise, you'll be dead. They could beat you to death. They don't have to account for anybody. Or the blockalteste has a gun. He can shoot you on the spot. He doesn't have to account to his superiors. He doesn't have to mark your name, nothing, finished. And he did it. And they did it, often, he said. So if you want to be alive, that's what you have to do. And-- something else he said. Wherever you go, if you have a chance to pick a commando-- commando is a type of work where you can steal food or clothing, a little more clothing for the winter. That's what-- if you have a chance, pick that, even if it's harder work, because what's the big deal with the light work. If you have no food, you'll die anyway. This way, you have a better chance. And they said, you speak to people that went through all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after six, seven days, I figure around seven days, we had an appell in the middle of the day. So that was unusual, so we already knew something was going on. Like a 12 o'clock appell. And there was [INAUDIBLE]. They brought us bread. Not two slices-- gave everybody a half a loaf-- and more margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: We already knew something good is going to happen. We already knew about the crematorium, about the-- the gas chambers in Auschwitz. In fact, some prisoner showed us the-- the other end over there, that's where they burn the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So what could we lose? We knew it can only be better. If they want to burn us, they wouldn't give everybody half a loaf of bread. Excuse me for a minute. Is that how you found out about the crematoria, from other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: From other prisoners, right. The old prisoners, they showed us. And then they explained why the other people went to the right or the left. The left meant-- the right were people who could do work. And the left are people who are old, disabled, children. There's no use for the German army to feed them. They can't produce anything, so that's the-- their end, right there. And they pointed to it. Although it was a few miles away, but we could see-- actually, we could see smoke coming out of the chimneys, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the middle of the day, there was an appell. We were used to appells, stand erect for hours. And they distributed bread. And then they said, everybody go into his barracks and take whatever is his, which was a spoon, maybe a knife or something. That's all we owned. We didn't own anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were marching about three, four kilometers, which is five, six miles, to a railroad track. And there was railroad cars waiting for us. At this time, they didn't put in 90, just 50 in a car, which wasn't bad. And there was an SS guard inside. And-- and after a while, after counting and recounting and checking the lists, and so on, the train took off. That was not a very long trip, I believe, a few hours. And we landed in Warsaw. OK. We got off. And our guards-- we had different guards, I don't remember. But we formed groups. We were marching through the-- the whole city of Warsaw, but fast. The Germans didn't want the Poles to see the brutality, you know, their brutality. They didn't want the civilian population to see, so we had to march fast. Anybody who couldn't keep up, they beat them. They didn't let them-- their policy was, in general, anybody who couldn't keep up in a march, leave him there or shoot him to death, or leave him there. Forget it. That was their policy, in general. That was their command. But in cities, they didn't do that. They didn't want the civilian population to see how they behave with the innocent people, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that you were in Warsaw? How did you know that you were in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sign said Warsaw. The railroad station said Warsaw. It was a regular station. It may not have been-- it was a regular station, and so we saw the sign, Warsaw. It was in Polish, probably in Polish. It's not W-A-R-S-A-W. But we read it. We read it and we knew it was in Warsaw. So-- and then we marched to a big area, a camp. But it was not an extermination camp. It was a labor camp. It was a labor camp. We-- we found that out within 24 hours from other prisoners who were there. And they said they had no gas chambers. But there's a lot of work, and hard work. And a few people don't make it, but most of them do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, in Warsaw, the first impression was very good. So at the gate-- it's a big camp, tremendous camp. At the gate, they had the same procedure. SS-- SS soldiers or officers made up lists, and counted and recounted. And then they assigned SS guards to-- to march them in to certain-- certain locations in the camp, certain barracks in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the barracks over there were clean. There was no comparison to-- to Auschwitz. And in the washrooms where we had to wash, there were-- it was regular plumbing. And it was very, very nice. Compared to what we saw in Auschwitz, this is just like heaven and hell. And then the food was a little more than in Auschwitz. And there was only one difference. Here, we had to perform hard work for this food. And-- and it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they read the regulations to us. The same system. Every barracks had a kapo and he gave us the orders. And-- and the barrack, the bunks were clean, as far as I remember, which is very unusual. Nice, clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember exactly. The food was better and there was more. And I think after one day, they-- they assigned us to work and smaller groups. And they explained what we have to do. One group was cleaning bricks. It's all bricks. The Jewish-- the Jewish ghetto was in the area, but the German planes destroyed the ghetto. And there was millions of bricks. So we prepared these bricks for shipment to Germany for construction. So before they went to Germany, they had to be cleaned up, so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Harry, I'll stop you.
